figures.caption: Figure 1: Part 1 illustrates the watermark generation process in LLMs, while Part 2 depicts the traditional Stealing Watermark Algorithm (SWA).

Table 1: Results of scrubbing attack for different watermarks and different scrubbing method. “Random” represents random classification during detection. Lower WCS, AUC, and TPR@1% values indicate better attack effectiveness.
Table 2: Results of spoofing attacks on different watermarks. w/o Attack represents the detectability performance of victim. Higher WCS, AUC, and TPR@1% values indicate better attack effectiveness.
Table 6: Results of spoofing attacks on KGW with different LMatt.

Table 3: Spoofing attack performance (AUC) across different watermark configurations using various seals. LMvic=Llama3-8b, and dataset is Dolly. no denotes the specific forged seal used for attack, “AVE” represents equally weighted ensemble of seals with no from 0 to 7. Left, Min and Max respectively indicates LeftHash-scheme, MinHash-scheme and MaxHash-scheme.
Figure 3: Trade-off between text quality (PPL) and watermark detectability (WCS) under spoofing attacks on KGW with varying δatt.
Figure 5: Results of spoofing attacks on KGW with different lengths of ctx.
Figure 2: The overall process of Adaptive Stealing (AS). Red arrows indicate the actions of AS, while black arrows indicate standard generation processes without the intervention of watermarking.
Figure 4: Results of spoofing attacks on KGW with varying |Dw|. The minimum |Dw| is 100.

Table 8: Spoofing attack result on different watermarks with WS as stealing watermark algorithm. The left three columns represent different settings of WS. Higher metric values indicate better attack performance.
